\subsection{Type Safety}
\label{sec:type}

Move has nominal typing: two structs with identical layout but different names are distinct types.
A \emph{type-confusion} exploit breaks that distinction, and can be very damaging when one of the two types is privileged.
Consider the example:

\begin{MoveBox}
module 0x1::admin {
  struct Capability(address);
  public fun admin_action(c: &Capability) {
    /* Capability gated action */
  }
}

module 0xdeadbeef::attacker {
  struct FakedCapability(address);
}
\end{MoveBox}

The attacker can freely construct a |FakedCapability| (they own the type); a type confusion exploit would allow passing it to |admin_action| as if it were a |Capability|.

\subsubsection{Example}
When vector operations were added to the VM during Move's original development at Meta, the bytecode verifier was not extended with the matching check on the vector element type for push.
The compiler did perform this check, so no input source program could reach the problem.
However, an attacker could bypass the compiler and directly construct bytecode, conceptually doing the following:

\begin{MoveBox}
let v: vector<Capability> = vector::empty();
v.push_back(FakedCapability(victim)); // should be rejected
admin_action(&v[0]);  // runs on a faked type
\end{MoveBox}

The two types share a layout, so program runs without corruption, but the gated action runs on a faked type.
This bug was found as part of the Aptos bug bounty program before going into production.

\subsubsection{Performing the Checks}
To prevent type confusion, in addition to the bytecode verifier, we perform type checks at runtime.
A type stack is maintained in parallel to the operand stack of the Move VM.
Because the Move VM's registers are typed, the type of every value is known: whenever a value is pushed on the operand stack, its type is pushed on the type stack.
Whenever a value is consumed, by being stored into a register or used by an operation, the corresponding type is popped and compared with the expected type of the register or operation; a mismatch is type confusion and raises a runtime error.

%$\theta ::= \epsilon \mid \theta \cdot \tau$ is added to the execution environment in Fig.~\ref{fig:semantics}.
%Let $r$ be a reference such that $r = (\textrm{index}(F), l)$ on $\COPYLOC l$ and $\MOVELOC l$, and the reference %on the stack for $\READREF$, then $\tau(r)$ is pushed on the type stack.
% When $\WRITEREF$ is executed, we pop the type $\tau'$ from the type stack and check $\tau' = \tau(r)$; if the check fails, type confusion has been detected.
